description: The uploaded image displays a generic user-interface badge labeled 'Check for updates' with a red bookmark icon inside a yellow-and-gray circular ring on a light-gray background. It contains no scientific data, sub-panels, micrographs, spectra, diffraction patterns, stress-strain curves, or any visual content pertinent to the investigated Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel. There are no visible coordinate axes, legends, scale bars, phase labels, grain structures, or material microstructural features. The accompanying text extraction summary references multiple figures from the paper—such as engineering stress-strain curves and strain-hardening-rate plots in Fig. 2, EBSD orientation and Schmid-factor maps, TEM images of dislocation cells and deformation twins, XRD profiles in Fig. 11, and Charpy impact fracture surfaces in Fig. 9—but none of these can be linked to or verified from the current image. Because the image is a non-scientific UI placeholder rather than a materials science figure, no sample conditions, processing parameters, quantitative performance metrics, or deformation mechanisms can be visually extracted or confirmed.

Figure 1 presents the microstructure of the solution-treated low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel after 1050 °C for 2 h. Panels (a–c) are EBSD maps: (a) phase map (D = 10.8 μm) showing a γ-austenite matrix with minor secondary phase along grain boundaries; citation states the matrix is FCC with about 3 % BCC α-ferrite and numerous annealing twin boundaries (visible white lines). (b) KAM map reporting geometrically necessary dislocation density ρ = 1.6 × 10¹³ m⁻². (c) IPF map along the rolling direction revealing very weak texture. Panels (d–f) are selected area electron diffraction patterns: (d) [100]γ zone axis exhibiting weak superlattice spots indexed as 0-11SRO and satellite reflections adjacent to γ matrix spots, with [100]γ // [100]SRO labeled; (e) [110]γ matrix pattern with spots including 1-1-1γ, 00-2γ, and -11-1; and (f) [110]MC pattern identifying MC carbide reflections (1-1-1MC, 00-2MC, -11-1MC). Citation states the superlattice spots indicate L1₂-type short-range ordering (SRO) and satellites originate from solute fluctuations. Together, these data establish an equiaxed austenitic microstructure with low dislocation density, weak texture, annealing twins, SRO, and MC carbides, providing the structural basis for the deformation twinning mechanism.

Figure 2 comprehensively illustrates the mechanical response of a low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel (Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C) at room temperature (RT) and liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT). Panel (a) presents engineering stress-strain curves, where the red LNT curve lies distinctly above the black RT curve, indicating higher strength and larger uniform elongation under cryogenic conditions. Panel (b) shows true stress-strain curves and corresponding strain hardening rate curves; the RT specimen exhibits two-stage hardening, whereas the LNT specimen displays four characteristic stages labeled A through D, with a sustained higher work hardening rate. Panel (c) provides instrumented impact force-displacement and absorbed energy-displacement curves. The image records crack initiation energy Wm of 79.28 J and crack propagation energy Wp of 251.33 J for RT, and Wm of 99.19 J with Wp of 156.71 J for LNT. The caption and context note that comparable crack initiation energies but reduced crack propagation energy at LNT lower the total impact toughness from ~331 J to ~255 J. Together, the data demonstrate that cryogenic deformation markedly elevates strength and alters the strain hardening pathway while maintaining high toughness governed mainly by crack propagation resistance.

This line chart (Fig. 4) plots the evolution of average Schmid factors as a function of tensile engineering strain for an Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel at both room temperature (RT) and liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT). Four distinct curves are shown: RT {111}<110>γ (grey squares), RT {111}<112>γ (red circles), LNT {111}<110>γ (blue triangles), and LNT {111}<112>γ (green inverted triangles). At RT, the {111}<110>γ Schmid factor drops abruptly from approximately 0.438 to 0.424 by 3% strain and then remains nearly constant, while the {111}<112>γ factor decreases moderately and stabilizes near 0.434. In contrast, at LNT both {111}<110>γ and {111}<112>γ Schmid factors peak at roughly 10% strain (around 0.444 and 0.450, respectively) before declining continuously with further deformation, with the {111}<110>γ factor falling to about 0.410 at 55% strain. These divergent trends reveal a strong temperature and orientation dependence of resolved shear stress on active slip and twinning systems, providing quantitative evidence for the distinct deformation twinning mechanism and enhanced strain hardening observed under cryogenic conditions.

Figure 3 presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) inverse pole figures (IPFs) showing the texture evolution of austenite along the tensile axis (TA) in a Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel during tensile deformation at room temperature (RT) and liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT). Panel (a) displays IPFs at 0 %, 3 %, 28 %, and 44 % engineering strains at RT, while panel (b) shows IPFs at 3 %, 10 %, 28 %, and 55 % engineering strains at LNT. The color scales indicate multiples of random distribution (MRD), revealing that the initially weak austenite texture progressively intensifies with increasing strain under both conditions. At RT, the maximum MRD increases from 2.55 (0 %) to 4.81 (44 %), whereas at LNT it rises from 2.01 (3 %) to 6.68 (55 %). A strong <111>//TA fiber texture develops alongside a weaker <100>//TA component. Caption states that while the <111>//TA texture strength at RT is slightly higher than at LNT at equivalent strains, the texture intensity at maximum uniform elongation is significantly stronger at LNT due to the higher achievable tensile strain (55 % vs 44 %), supporting enhanced deformation twinning and strain hardening at cryogenic temperatures.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) analysis revealing the orientation dependence of deformation twinning in a low-carbon low-density steel during tensile deformation at room temperature (RT) and liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT). The top row (a1–d1) displays inverse pole figures (IPFs) along the tensile axis (TA), where red, green, and blue dots denote Type I, II, and III grain orientations, respectively, with regions satisfying m_tw < m_s indicated. The bottom row (a2–d2) shows corresponding EBSD inverse pole maps capturing microstructural evolution at matched conditions. At RT 38% strain (a1, a2), deformation twins are scarce, whereas at LNT the twin fraction increases progressively from 28% to 55% strain (b1–d1, b2–d2). Type II and III grains exhibit a markedly higher propensity for twinning than Type I grains. Specifically, Type II grains tend to form single-orientation twins, while Type III grains favor multiple-orientation twins. Although Type III grains follow Schmid law with m_tw > m_s, Type II grains undergo extensive twinning despite m_tw < m_s, indicating that cryogenic deformation twinning is strongly grain-orientation-dependent but cannot be fully explained by Schmid factor alone.

The figure presents EBSD characterization of dislocation-glide-induced grain subdivision in Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C steel deformed to 28 % engineering strain at room temperature (RT; a–c) and liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT; d–f). Subfigures (a) and (d) are inverse pole figure (IPF) maps along the tensile axis (TA), displaying grain orientations overlaid with indexed crystallographic slip traces such as (111) and (11-1). Subfigures (b) and (e) are kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps with a 0°–5° scale, revealing geometrically necessary dislocation distributions that delineate active slip bands and evolving subgrain boundaries. Subfigures (c) and (f) are standard inverse pole figures along TA that plot sub-grain orientations according to slip mode: green dots indicate single glide and dark blue/black dots indicate double glide, with boundaries demarcating regions where the twinning Schmid factor exceeds the slip Schmid factor (m_tw > m_s) versus where it is lower (m_tw < m_s). The comparison between RT and LNT conditions demonstrates pronounced orientation-dependent activation of single versus double slip systems during grain subdivision, supporting the conclusion that dislocation glide is the dominant microstructural refinement mechanism at this strain level in both temperature regimes.

This figure presents a series of TEM bright-field images with corresponding SAED patterns illustrating the strain-dependent evolution of deformation microstructures in a low-carbon low-density Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C steel during room-temperature tensile testing. Subpanel (a) at 3 % strain shows planar-slip-related dislocation nodes, multi-junctions, and dislocation pairs on {111} planes. Subpanels (b, c) at 10 % reveal Lomer-Cottrell locks and high-density dislocation walls. Subpanels (d–f) at 20 % exhibit Taylor lattices, cell blocks (CBs), and dislocation cells (DCs), where type I grains oriented near <100> // tensile axis favor uniform DCs while type II/III grains develop CBs separated by cell block walls (CBWs). At 28 % (g–j), CBs and DCs refine significantly and deformation twins nucleate along CBWs, accompanied by microbands indicating stress localization. Subpanels (k, l) at 38 % show twins further developed along primary slip planes. Collectively, the images provide direct evidence that deformation twinning occurs in this high-stacking-fault-energy steel at large strains, supported by stress concentration arising from planar dislocation substructures.

This figure presents a comprehensive TEM investigation of deformation-induced microstructures in an Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel subjected to tensile loading at liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT) across engineering strains from 3% to 55%. Panels (a–b) at 3% reveal dislocation nodes, dislocation pairs, dislocation multi-junctions, and stacking faults at annealed twin boundaries. At 10% strain, panels (c–e) show pronounced planar slip traces and the nucleation of deformation twins with bundle widths of approximately 5 nm at annealed twin boundaries. Panels (f–i) at 20% display dislocation cell blocks and dislocation cells alongside sparsely distributed twins (~24 nm wide), indicating competition between dislocation glide and twinning. At 28% strain, panels (j–o) exhibit refined cell blocks, wide microbands (~390 nm), and fine microbands (~137 nm). Panels (p–q) at 38% demonstrate extensive activation of deformation twins. At 55% strain, panels (r–t) reveal primary twins and secondary twins containing abundant stacking faults. SAED insets confirm the crystallographic nature of these features. Collectively, the figure supports that low temperature facilitates planar glide and stacking fault formation at early strains, followed by sequential activation of twins, microbands, and secondary twinning, which underlies the sustained strain hardening at cryogenic temperatures.

This figure comparatively displays the deformation microstructures and fracture features of a low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel following impact testing at room temperature (RT, a–e) and liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT, f–j). Macroscopic fracture appearances (a, f) show extensive necking and abundant subordinate cracks for the RT sample, contrasting with limited necking and fewer subordinate cracks at LNT. EBSD phase maps (b, g) captured near the fracture surface indicate a stable austenite (γ) matrix containing minor ferrite (α); red arrows in (g) highlight fine cracks encircling voids from detached α-phase particles, suggesting the γ/α interface serves as a crack initiation site during cryogenic impact. EBSD IPF maps along the impact direction (c, h) visualize increased deformation twinning activity at LNT. TEM bright-field images (d, i) taken ~1000 μm beneath the fracture surface reveal twins and microbands, with corresponding SAED patterns inset. SEM fractographs (e, j) exhibit micrometer- to sub-micrometer-scale dimples at both temperatures, confirming ductile fracture. The data collectively indicate that although low temperature favors deformation twinning, interfacial decohesion at γ/α boundaries contributes to reduced crack propagation energy, while the fracture mode remains ductile across both test temperatures.

This figure presents a comparative evaluation of the studied Fe35Mn6Al0.1C low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel against multiple reported alloys tested at liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT), using specific yield strength as the common abscissa. Panel (a) plots Charpy impact energy versus specific yield strength, whereas panel (b) plots total elongation versus specific yield strength. The "Present work" (red circle) is benchmarked against low-carbon and high-carbon lightweight steels, low-carbon high-manganese steel, TWIP steel, 304 stainless steel, 9Ni steel, and the Al0.1CoCrFeNi high-entropy alloy. At a specific yield strength near 70 MPa·cm³·g⁻¹, the designed steel exhibits a Charpy impact energy of approximately 250 J and a total elongation near 75%, which caption states correspond to 254 ± 6 J and 75.5 ± 0.5%. It surpasses high-carbon lightweight and TWIP steels in impact toughness, exceeds low-carbon high-Mn and 304 stainless steels in specific yield strength, and delivers ductility nearly three times higher than 9Ni steel. These data collectively demonstrate that the steel combines low density, excellent cryogenic toughness, superior ductility, and cost effectiveness, establishing it as a highly competitive candidate for cryogenic applications.

Figure 11 evaluates austenite phase stability in a low-carbon low-density steel (Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C) through XRD examination and phase-diagram calculation. Panel (a) displays XRD profiles of specimens after tensile deformation at liquid nitrogen temperature to strains of 0 %, 3 %, 10 %, 28 %, and 55 %. The patterns show prominent FCC austenite reflections—(111)γ, (200)γ, and (220)γ—alongside a weak (110)α ferrite peak. Quantified austenite volume fractions remain stable at about 95.6 %–97.3 % across all strains, indicating negligible deformation-induced martensitic transformation even under severe cryogenic deformation. Panel (b) presents a thermodynamic calculation using Thermal-Calc software (TCFE12 database), plotting molar fractions of ferrite (red curve) and austenite (blue curve) versus temperature. An arrow marks the solution-treatment region near 1050 °C, where the predicted ferrite fraction is near zero and austenite dominates, consistent with the experimentally observed high retained-austenite stability. Collectively, the data demonstrate that the steel maintains exceptional austenite stability at cryogenic temperatures, thereby supporting deformation twinning as the primary plasticity mechanism rather than a TRIP effect.

This schematic stereographic triangle illustrates the Taylor factor (M) distribution as a function of crystallographic orientation along the tensile axis (TA) direction for the investigated Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel. The triangular orientation map is bounded by the standard [001]–[101]–[111] directions, with annotated M values of 2.30 at the [001] corner and 3.64 at both the [101] and [111] corners. Two dashed-boundary twinning regions are delineated: one near the [001] vertex labeled 'M < 2.7' and another toward the [101]–[111] edge labeled 'M > 3.06', both marked with 'Twins'. The arrow labeled 'M' indicates increasing Taylor factor from [001] toward the [101]–[111] side. The caption and cited text state that deformation twinning is primarily activated in high-Taylor-factor grains (type II and III, associated with <111>//TA and <110>//TA orientations with M up to 3.64), whereas low-M grains (type I, near <001>//TA with M ≈ 2.30–2.7) show limited twinning. This sketch supports the core conclusion that stacking fault energy is not the sole decisive factor governing twinning; the crystallographic orientation-dependent Taylor factor plays a critical role in the deformation twinning mechanism of this austenitic steel at cryogenic temperatures.
